You said: I remember learning Sanskrit in Maharasthra board 8-10th standard textbooks back in ~2006

I remember learning Sanskrit in Maharasthra board 8-10th standard textbooks back in ~2006

the way it was taught was very weird, we had to do a ratta(rote learning) of nouns like देवः देवौ देवाः (and I think) these were only nouns and not verbs.

heck I don't think I remember anything about the verbs. I do remember about the समास (like बहुव्रीहि समास) but nothing about the verbs.

Now, I am not actively trying to learn Sanskrit, but as you might already know, I have an interest in languages. I came across a thing where they are trying to teach Sanskrit via Bhagavad Geeta. Here are the initial chapters - 1. https://gita-sanskrit-teacher.netlify.app/ 2. https://bvsiitm.github.io/sanskrit-gita-learn/ 3. https://bvsiitm.github.io/sanskrit-gita-learn/interlude 4. https://bvsiitm.github.io/sanskrit-gita-learn/lesson/2

Where I am going with this - I want to learn more about the verbs. The links above mention prathama vibhakti etc.

Tangentially related: I have tried to read Bibek Debroy's "Bhagavad Gita for Millenials" and I had created some notes about SOV, SVO thingies which I have attached here.

Mar 14

Claude responded: What a fun rabbit hole!

Synthesized Sanskrit grammar knowledge to bridge noun-verb learning gap

What a fun rabbit hole! Your notes are great — you've already absorbed more than you think. Let me connect the dots between what you know (nouns/विभक्ति) and what you want to understand (verbs), using the Gita context.

The thing your school Sanskrit missed

Your school taught you noun declension tables — देवः, देवौ, देवाः — which are the 8 विभक्तियाँ (cases) × 3 वचन (numbers). That's the noun system.

Verbs have a parallel system, but instead of cases, you have person × number, and it's driven by the धातु (root).

Verbs: The Core Idea

Every Sanskrit verb comes from a धातु (root). The most famous one in the Gita:

धातुMeaning
√कृto do
√भूto be
√गम्to go
√युध्to fight

The verb form tells you who is doing it and how many — no separate pronoun needed (just like in Hindi: "जाता हूँ" vs "जाता है").

The 3×3 Grid (the verb equivalent of देवः/देवौ/देवाः)

Sanskrit has 3 पुरुष (persons) × 3 वचन (numbers) = 9 verb forms per tense.

⚠️ Confusing quirk: Sanskrit's प्रथम पुरुष = English 3rd person (he/she/it). It's counted from the outside in, not like English.

पुरुषएकवचनद्विवचनबहुवचन
प्रथम (he/she/it)करोतिकुरुतःकुर्वन्ति
मध्यम (you)करोषिकुरुथःकुरुथ
उत्तम (I/we)करोमिकुर्वःकुर्मः

This is √कृ in लट् लकार (simple present tense).

The connection to प्रथमा विभक्ति

Here's the satisfying link to what you already know:

  • प्रथमा विभक्ति (nominative case) marks the subject of the sentence
  • The subject's वचन (singular/dual/plural) must match the verb's पुरुष-वचन form

So in the very first verse of the Gita:

धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय

Your notes already broke this down! किमकुर्वत = किम् + अकुर्वत

अकुर्वत = they did (प्रथम पुरुष, बहुवचन, past tense of √कृ) ...matching मामकाः पाण्डवाः च which is बहुवचन, प्रथमा विभक्ति. ✓

The Tense System: लकार

The "tenses" in Sanskrit are called लकार, named after the suffix used. The main ones you'll hit in the Gita:

लकारUsageExample
लट्Presentकरोति — he does
लङ्Past (imperfect)अकरोत् — he did
लृट्Futureकरिष्यति — he will do
लोट्Imperativeकरोतु — let him do / do!
विधिलिङ्Should/ought toकुर्यात् — he should do

Your notes mention अकुर्वत is in imperfect past tense and plural — that's लङ् लकार, and you were right on the money.

Why this matters for the Gita specifically

The Gita is very verb-rich. Krishna's instructions are mostly in लोट् (imperatives) and विधिलिङ् (ought-to forms):

योगः कर्मसु कौशलम् — skill in action is yoga
कुर्वन्नेवेह कर्माणि — doing actions here itself...

The participle forms (like कुर्वन् = "the one doing") are another big thing in the Gita — they come from the same धातु but act like adjectives.

Practical suggestion for those links

The sites you shared are teaching through सन्धि → पदच्छेद → अन्वय — exactly what your notes cover. Once you're in those lessons and see a verb form, the question to ask is:

  1. What's the धातु? (They'll usually tell you)
  2. What लकार? (Present/past/future/imperative)
  3. What पुरुष + वचन? (Who's doing it, how many)

That three-step decode cracks most Gita verbs open. The छंद/metre notes you made are a bonus — once you internalize अनुष्टुभ's rhythm, you start hearing where words must end, which helps with पदच्छेद immensely.

You said: BTW (a little Tangential (to give some context) (don't go on a wild goose chase)) - this देवः देवौ देवाः was taught from a book called नवनीतs शब्द धातु रूपावलि…

BTW (a little Tangential (to give some context) (don't go on a wild goose chase)) - this देवः देवौ देवाः was taught from a book called नवनीतs शब्द धातु रूपावलिः - and while I could recite a lot of देवः देवौ देवाः by heart, it was a pain for the others. Could you shed some light on the book?

Mar 14

Claude responded: Ha, that book!

Contextualized Sanskrit textbook reference and acknowledged knowledge limits

Ha, that book! Very specifically a Maharashtra thing. Here's what I can tell you without overreaching:

Navneet (नवनीत) as a publisher

Navneet Publications is a Mumbai-based educational publisher, deeply embedded in the Maharashtra SSC board ecosystem. They make two kinds of things:

  • Guides (the famous "digest" — every Maharashtra student knows these)
  • Reference booklets like the शब्द धातु रूपावलि

The रूपावलि booklet was essentially a lookup table / drill book — thin, cheap, and comprehensive in a very specific way. It listed noun paradigms (शब्द रूप) and verb conjugations (धातु रूप) in exhaustive tables, meant purely for memorization.

Why देवः was "easy" and others were painful

देवः is the अकारान्त पुल्लिंग paradigm — masculine nouns ending in -अ. It's the Sanskrit equivalent of a "regular verb" in French — once you know it, you know the template for hundreds of words.

The painful ones were likely:

ParadigmExampleWhy painful
इकारान्त पुल्लिंगकविDifferent endings throughout
उकारान्तसाधुAnother set of endings
ऋकारान्तपितृFeels almost unrelated
व्यञ्जनान्त (consonant stems)राजन्, आत्मन्Stem itself changes
स्त्रीलिंगरमा, नदी, मतिCompletely different sets

And then verbs — the धातु रूप side of the book — had 10 लकार × 9 forms × multiple धातु which is... a lot of rote learning for a 14-year-old.

The core pedagogical problem

The रूपावलि approach taught you the outputs without the logic.

देवः देवौ देवाः — you memorized that. But why does the plural become देवाः? That's the विसर्ग rule + plural nominative suffix. Nobody explained that because the exam didn't ask for it.

Compare to what your Gita links are doing — they're teaching you to derive forms from roots, which is how Sanskrit was actually meant to be learned (through Panini's Ashtadhyayi framework, where rules generate forms rather than tables listing them).

The honest gap it left

You got noun shapes without verb logic, which means you could recognize who/what in a sentence but not what's happening. That's actually a very specific and fixable gap — which is exactly where your current interest is pointed. The ratta wasn't useless; you internalized the feel of Sanskrit morphology. You just never got the verb side of the same coin.

You said: anything you want to add more?

anything you want to add more? (don't go off the rails though)

Mar 14

Claude responded: One genuinely interesting irony worth noting:

Weighed ironic pedagogical tensions within Sanskrit grammar systems

One genuinely interesting irony worth noting:

Panini's Ashtadhyayi — the ~2500-year-old grammar that describes Sanskrit — is essentially a generative rule system. Linguists and computer scientists consider it one of the most sophisticated formal grammars ever written, predating modern computational linguistics by millennia. It derives forms from roots using rules, almost like a program.

And yet, Maharashtra SSC taught Sanskrit via its exact opposite — a table of pre-generated outputs with zero derivation logic.

You were handed the compiled binary and asked to memorize it, when the source code was sitting right there.

The other small thing: नवनीत literally means butter (fresh, soft — same root as नवनीत in Hindi/Marathi). It's a slightly charming name for a book that was, for most students, anything but.

You said: I am going through this chapter

I am going through this chapter https://bvsiitm.github.io/sanskrit-gita-learn/lesson/2

For a Questoin -  What does "वृन्दावने कृष्णः (Bṛndāvane Kṛṣṇaḥ)" mean?

Kṛṣṇa is in Bṛndāvana Kṛṣṇa goes to Bṛndāvana Kṛṣṇa is from Bṛndāvana Kṛṣṇa belongs to Bṛndāvana

I chose - Kṛṣṇa is in Bṛndāvana Correct. Bṛndāvane is saptamī — "in Bṛndāvana". Kṛṣṇaḥ is nominative subject.

What does "nominative subject" mean here? Any parallels to English grammar that I might be familiar with

Mar 14

Claude responded: Nominative = the "who" case — the noun that is the subject of the sentence.

Identified Sanskrit grammar concept and English language parallels

Nominative = the "who" case — the noun that is the subject of the sentence.

In English you don't really see it because English nouns don't change shape. But pronouns do, and that's your familiar example:

Nominative (subject)Accusative (object)
He hit the ballI hit him
She calledI called her
They leftI saw them

"He/She/They" are nominative. The word changes shape when it stops being the subject.

Sanskrit does this for every noun, not just pronouns. So:

  • कृष्णः — Krishna (as subject, nominative)
  • कृष्णम् — Krishna (as object, accusative)

Same noun, different ending, different grammatical role. That's exactly the देवः → देवम् thing from your रूपावलि days — देवः is nominative, देवम् is accusative.

The case your lesson just taught you — सप्तमी (Saptamī) — has no real equivalent in English because English just uses prepositions instead:

  • Sanskrit changes the word: वृन्दावन → वृन्दावने (the -े ending means "in")
  • English adds a separate word: "in Vrindavan"

That's the core difference. Sanskrit bakes the preposition into the noun ending.

You said: what case is "वृन्दावने"

what case is "वृन्दावने"

Mar 14

Claude responded: सप्तमी (Saptamī) — the locative case, meaning "in/at/on".

Identified grammatical case through noun declension analysis

सप्तमी (Saptamī) — the locative case, meaning "in/at/on".

I actually mentioned this one line above in my previous answer! The -े ending on वृन्दावन is what signals it.

In your रूपावलि terms: it's the 7th विभक्ति, singular. देव → देवे follows the exact same pattern.

You said: can you give all the 8 things (प्रथम (1st)/सप्तमी(7th)) and that एकवचन/द्विवचन/बहुवचन in English

can you give all the 8 things (प्रथम (1st)/सप्तमी(7th)) and that एकवचन/द्विवचन/बहुवचन in English

Mar 14

Claude responded: 8 विभक्ति (Cases)

Identified request for Sanskrit grammar cases and numbers

8 विभक्ति (Cases)

#NameCaseMeaning
1प्रथमाNominativeSubject — who is doing
2द्वितीयाAccusativeDirect object — what is being acted on
3तृतीयाInstrumentalBy/with — by means of
4चतुर्थीDativeFor/to — for whom
5पञ्चमीAblativeFrom — away from
6षष्ठीGenitiveOf/possession — whose
7सप्तमीLocativeIn/at/on — where
8सम्बोधनVocativeDirect address — hey you!

3 वचन (Numbers)

NameEnglishMeaning
एकवचनSingularone
द्विवचनDualexactly two
बहुवचनPluralthree or more

The dual is the interesting one — English lost it (we just say "both"), but Sanskrit requires you to be precise about whether you mean 2 or 3+. Your रूपावलि had all three columns for exactly this reason.

You said: what's that स्त्रीलिंग पुल्लिंग नपुसकलिंग that I remember reading about

what's that स्त्रीलिंग पुल्लिंग नपुसकलिंग that I remember reading about

Mar 14

Claude responded: Grammatical Gender (लिंग)

Identified Sanskrit's three grammatical genders from user's recollection

Grammatical Gender (लिंग)

NameGenderMeaning
पुल्लिंगMasculinehe
स्त्रीलिंगFeminineshe
नपुंसकलिंगNeuterit

Not much more to it conceptually. The painful part is that grammatical gender ≠ natural gender, just like in Hindi or French.

For example:

  • जल (water) — neuter
  • पुस्तक (book) — neuter
  • नदी (river) — feminine (same in Hindi)
  • राम — masculine (obvious)

The reason it mattered in your रूपावलि is that the same 8×3 case table has different endings depending on gender. So देवः (masc), रमा (fem), फलम् (neuter) — same 24 slots, three different sets of endings.

That's why the book had so many tables. It wasn't 8×3 = 24 forms to memorize. It was more like 24 × 3 genders × multiple stem-types = the pain you remember.

You said: Do ramcharitmanas verses also follow sanskrit i.

Do ramcharitmanas verses also follow sanskrit i.e. no SOV or SVO and depend upon the vibhakti thinfy e.g.

मसक समान रूप कपि धरी। लंकहि चलेउ सुमिरि नरहरी॥ नाम लंकिनी एक निसिचरी। सो कह चलेसि मोहि निंदरी॥

Mar 14

Claude responded: Short answer: No — Ramcharitmanas is not Sanskrit.

Differentiated Awadhi from Sanskrit, examined postpositional case markers

Short answer: No — Ramcharitmanas is not Sanskrit. It's written in Awadhi (अवधी), a Middle Indo-Aryan language closer to old Hindi.

What that means practically

Awadhi doesn't have the full Sanskrit vibhakti system. Instead it uses postpositions — separate little words/suffixes tacked on — just like modern Hindi's को, से, में, के लिए.

In your verse, लंकहि — that -हि suffix is Awadhi's way of saying "to Lanka". It's doing a vibhakti-like job but it's not Sanskrit vibhakti. It's a postposition baked onto the word.

So word order?

More fixed than Sanskrit, but more flexible than modern Hindi — roughly SOV leaning, but Tulsidas bends it freely for meter and rhyme (it's poetry after all, in चौपाई metre).

The lineage simply put

Sanskrit → Prakrits → Apabhramsha → Awadhi/Braj/etc → Modern Hindi

Ramcharitmanas sits in the middle of that chain — Sanskrit ancestry is visible (vocabulary, some case echoes) but the grammar is already moving away from vibhakti toward postpositions.

Tulsidas deliberately chose Awadhi over Sanskrit to reach common people — same content as Valmiki's Ramayana, democratized.

You said: So how does one do अन्वय and पदच्छेद

So how does one do अन्वय and पदच्छेद

Mar 14

Claude responded: These are Sanskrit tools — they don't apply cleanly to Ramcharitmanas because Awadhi doesn't have the vibhakti endings that make them work.

Clarified linguistic parsing techniques across Sanskrit and Awadhi contexts

These are Sanskrit tools — they don't apply cleanly to Ramcharitmanas because Awadhi doesn't have the vibhakti endings that make them work.

For Sanskrit specifically:

पदच्छेद (Word Splitting)

Sanskrit compounds words and sandhi-joins them aggressively. पदच्छेद is just separating them back out.

Your notes already had this:

धर्मक्षेत्रे → धर्म + क्षेत्रे

That's पदच्छेद. You're undoing सन्धि and समास to get individual words.

अन्वय (Logical Ordering)

After पदच्छेद, you reorder the words into a parseable sequence:

Subject → Object → Verb

And because Sanskrit has vibhakti, you know who is subject (प्रथमा ending), who is object (द्वितीया ending), regardless of where they sit in the original verse.

So the steps are:

  1. पदच्छेद — split the words
  2. Identify vibhakti endings — find subject/object/etc
  3. अन्वय — reorder into Subject+Object+Verb
  4. Translate

The vibhakti system is precisely what makes अन्वय possible — because endings carry meaning, word order is free to be scrambled for metre. अन्वय is just unscrambling it.

You said: Okay but Awadhi verses also don't seem to follow the SOV/SVO so how does one understand what is being said

Okay but Awadhi verses also don't seem to follow the SOV/SVO so how does one understand what is being said

Mar 14

Claude responded: Good observation.

Examined word order flexibility in Awadhi poetry comprehension

Good observation. A few things are doing the work instead of vibhakti:

What holds Awadhi poetry together

1. Context + common sense

The biggest one honestly. You know Hanuman is going to Lanka, not the other way around. Real ambiguity is rare because the story constrains interpretation.

2. Postpositions still signal roles

Even when scrambled, the -हि, -कहँ, -सों etc. travel with their noun. So लंकहि ("to Lanka") is identifiable wherever it sits in the line.

3. Verb is almost always at the end

Even in poetry, Awadhi broadly respects SOV at the clause level. Tulsidas scrambles within that but rarely puts the verb mid-line.

4. The reader/listener already knows the story

Ramcharitmanas was performed — sung, recited. The audience knew the Ramayana. So they weren't parsing cold, they were following along a known narrative.

Honest answer though

A lot of Ramcharitmanas comprehension is just built up through exposure — the same way you understand Shakespeare's inverted sentences not by parsing grammar rules but by reading enough of it that the register becomes familiar.

It's less linguistics, more immersion.

You said: Give examples

Give examples

Mar 14

Claude responded: Using your own verse:

Identified linguistic framework to illustrate Awadhi poetry comprehension

Using your own verse:

मसक समान रूप कपि धरी। लंकहि चलेउ सुमिरि नरहरी॥

Step 1 — postposition does the work:

  • लंकहि = to Lanka (-हि signals direction)
  • So whatever the verb is, the motion is toward Lanka

Step 2 — verb at end of clause:

  • चलेउ = went/set off (end of second quarter)
  • धरी = having taken/assumed (end of first quarter)

Step 3 — context fills the rest:

  • कपि = monkey (Hanuman, obviously, given the story)
  • मसक समान रूप = mosquito-like form
  • नरहरी = Vishnu/Ram (सुमिरि = having remembered)

So even though the order is scrambled, you reconstruct: The monkey assumed a mosquito-sized form, remembered Ram, and set off to Lanka.

नाम लंकिनी एक निसिचरी। सो कह चलेसि मोहि निंदरी॥

  • नाम = named (a frozen formula — "there was one called...")
  • निसिचरी = female night-demon (gender ending still visible, Sanskrit ancestry)
  • सो कह = she said (सो = she, kah = said — SV right there)
  • चलेसि = you go/went
  • मोहि निंदरी = having disrespected me (मोहि = me, postposition-like)

Again — if you know the story, you know Lankins is the guardian challenging Hanuman. The grammar just confirms what context already told you.

The pattern is basically: postpositions anchor the nouns, verb anchors the clause end, story anchors everything else.

You said: Give other examples than one I gave

Give other examples than one I gave

E.g. प्रबिसि नगर कीजे सब काजा। हृदयँ राखि कोसलपुर राजा॥ गरल सुधा रिपु करहिं मिताई। गोपद सिंधु अनल सितलाई॥

Mar 14

Claude responded: प्रबिसि नगर कीजे सब काजा। हृदयँ राखि कोसलपुर राजा॥

Identified request to furnish alternative Awadhi verse examples

प्रबिसि नगर कीजे सब काजा। हृदयँ राखि कोसलपुर राजा॥

Verb at clause end:

  • प्रबिसि = having entered (absolutive — "enter and then...")
  • कीजे = do/accomplish (imperative, end of first half)
  • राखि = having kept (another absolutive)

Postposition doing work:

  • हृदयँ = in the heart (the -ँ is locative echo, Awadhi's version of सप्तमी)

Context fills rest:

  • नगर = the city (Lanka, obviously — Hanuman is mid-mission)
  • सब काजा = all the tasks/work
  • कोसलपुर राजा = the king of Kosala (Ram)

So: Enter the city and accomplish all tasks, keeping the king of Kosala in your heart.

This is someone giving Hanuman instructions. Without knowing that, "enter the city, do all work" is a bit floating — context anchors it completely.

गरल सुधा रिपु करहिं मिताई। गोपद सिंधु अनल सितलाई॥

This one is more interesting — no postpositions at all. It works entirely through:

Parallel structure / poetic formula: Each half follows the same pattern:

  • Noun1 + Noun2 + verb/result
Noun 1Noun 2Result
गरल (poison)सुधा (nectar)करहिं मिताई (become friends)
गोपद (cow's hoofprint puddle)सिंधु (ocean)—
अनल (fire)सितलाई (coolness)—

Once you see the parallel, the second line parses itself — a puddle becomes the ocean, fire becomes cool.

Meaning: For a devotee of Ram, poison becomes nectar, a puddle becomes the ocean, fire becomes cool — the impossible becomes possible.

The second verse is a good example of how poetic parallelism itself becomes a parsing tool — once you crack the pattern of the first pair, the structure tells you how to read the rest, even without postpositions or clear SOV.

You said: What do you mean by the poetic parallelism pattern?

What do you mean by the poetic parallelism pattern?

Mar 14

Claude responded: Look at the verse again:

Prepared explanation of poetic parallelism with illustrative examples

Look at the verse again:

गरल सुधा रिपु करहिं मिताई। गोपद सिंधु अनल सितलाई॥

Once you parse the first pair:

गरल + सुधा + करहिं मिताई = poison + nectar + become friends

You now have a template:

[impossible thing 1] + [impossible thing 2] + [impossible relationship between them]

The second line gives you three more nouns — गोपद, सिंधु, अनल — and one result word — सितलाई (coolness).

You didn't get a verb this time. But the template from line 1 tells you:

  • These nouns must be fitting the same pattern
  • गोपद (tiny puddle) and सिंधु (ocean) are the impossible pair
  • अनल (fire) + सितलाई (coolness) is the third impossible pair
  • The verb करहिं is implied/borrowed from line 1

The key point

You're not parsing word-by-word anymore. You're recognizing a shape — and filling in what must logically go where.

It's similar to how in English if someone says:

"Cats bark, dogs meow, fire freezes, ice burns"

You don't need grammar explanation — the parallel rhythm itself tells you each pair is an inversion of reality.

Tulsidas does this constantly. It's a feature, not a shortcut.

You said: Any papers that you'd recommend to read about such readings of ramcharitmanas for meaning/अनवय/पदचेछद etc

Any papers that you'd recommend to read about such readings of ramcharitmanas for meaning/अनवय/पदचेछद etc

Mar 14

Claude responded: Honestly, I'd rather not throw paper titles at you here — this is an area where I'd risk confidently hallucinating citations, which would waste your time.

Weighed citation confidence and opted for honest scholarly guidance

Honestly, I'd rather not throw paper titles at you here — this is an area where I'd risk confidently hallucinating citations, which would waste your time.

What I'm confident enough to point you toward:

Philip Lutgendorf — The Life of a Text

This is a real, well-known academic book about Ramcharitmanas — specifically about how it's been read, performed, and interpreted over centuries. Not purely linguistic but very relevant to your question of how meaning is constructed.

Ramchandra Shukla (रामचंद्र शुक्ल)

His Hindi criticism on Tulsidas is considered foundational. Written in Hindi, so accessible to you. Search for "गोस्वामी तुलसीदास" by Shukla.

Better directions than papers:

  • Banaras Hindu University has a Sanskrit/Hindi department that produces this kind of work — their journals would be the right place to search
  • Search terms: "Awadhi grammar", "Tulsidas linguistic analysis", "Ramcharitmanas commentary tradition"
  • Traditional टीका (commentary) literature on Ramcharitmanas is arguably more useful than modern papers for what you want — scholars like Hanuman Prasad Poddar wrote extensive line-by-line commentaries

Do you want me to do a live search and see what actually comes up?

You said: Yes, there must be 100s who do their PhDs on manas.

Yes, there must be 100s who do their PhDs on manas. You must be able to find something useful

Mar 14

Claude responded: Okay, this was actually a productive search.

Synthesized scholarly sources on epic textual analysis and linguistics

Okay, this was actually a productive search. Here's what's genuinely useful for your specific interest:

The one person who matters most: Philip Lutgendorf

He translated the Ramcharitmanas into English in seven volumes for the Murty Classical Library of India (Harvard University Press). This is the most relevant thing for you — dual-language, scholarly, verse-by-verse. The Murty Classical Library editions are specifically designed to be linguistically accessible. Wikipedia

He also published a "Ramcaritmanas Word Index / Manas Shabda Anukramanika" (with Winand Callewaert), published by Manohar, New Delhi (1997). This is essentially a concordance — every word in the Manas, indexed. Directly useful for your kind of close reading. Uiowa

His 1989 article "The View from the Ghats: Traditional Exegesis of a Hindu Epic" (Journal of Asian Studies, 48, pp. 272–88) is specifically about the टीका/commentary tradition — how pandits have historically constructed meaning from the text. That's the closest thing to your अन्वय question applied to Manas.

The practical starting point though

Before any papers — The Life of a Text covers metrical and narrative structure in chapter 1, which is where the meaning-construction discussion actually lives in accessible form. Google Books

For the word-level stuff, the Murty Library volumes are your best bet — they're annotated translations, not just raw text.

You said: coming back to sanskrit, there's this famous AI guy who tweets about Sanskrit - https://x.

coming back to sanskrit, there's this famous AI guy who tweets about Sanskrit - https://x.com/khoomeik

He recently uploaded an image (which I am attaching with this message)

I want to know if you could shed some light into what he is saying. That dhatus/verbs that he is saying - is that diff from the nouns and all the 8x3 forms like we had for देवः देवौ देवाः

Mar 14
2 / 2

Claude responded: Yes, completely different system — same idea (a root that generates forms) but for verbs instead of nouns.

Parsed Sanskrit verb roots versus noun declension systems

Yes, completely different system — same idea (a root that generates forms) but for verbs instead of nouns.

What the image is showing

These are धातु (dhātu) — verb roots. The most fundamental building blocks of Sanskrit. Everything in the chart is a root, not a full word. For example:

  • √भू — to be/exist (the most common one, top-left)
  • √वच् — to speak/tell
  • √कृ — to do/make (the one from your Gita verse — अकुर्वत)
  • √गम् — to go

These roots by themselves mean nothing in a sentence — they need suffixes added to become actual verb forms.

The parallel to देवः देवौ देवाः

NounsVerbs
Rootदेव (base)√कृ (dhātu)
What variesCase + Number (8×3)Person + Number + Tense (3×3×10 लकार)
Exampleदेवः / देवम् / देवेकरोति / कुरुतः / कुर्वन्ति
SignalEnding tells you role in sentenceEnding tells you who's doing it + when

Same logic, different dimension. Nouns answer "who/what/where", verbs answer "who does it/when".

Why 192 specifically

This is from the Digital Corpus of Sanskrit — a computational analysis of actual Sanskrit texts. So these aren't all possible dhātus (Panini lists ~2000), just the 192 that appear most frequently in real texts. Very practical list if you're reading the Gita.

Notice √भू, √कृ, √वच्, √गम्, √स्था are at the very top — these are your workhorses, exactly the ones you'll keep hitting in the Gita.

The color coding

The colors at the bottom of the image indicate गण (gaṇa) — verb class. Sanskrit verbs are grouped into 10 classes based on how they form their present tense stem. It's roughly analogous to verb conjugation groups in French/Spanish. Same dhātu, but the gaṇa tells you how the suffix attaches.

This is the one thing that makes Sanskrit verbs genuinely hard — you need to know both the root and its class to correctly conjugate it.

The honest practical note

For reading the Gita, you don't need all 192. The top ~20-30 cover an enormous portion of the text. √कृ, √भू, √गम्, √वद्, √ज्ञा, √दृश् — learn these and you've already decoded a huge chunk.

You said: elaborate about the 10 ganas

elaborate about the 10 ganas

Mar 14

Claude responded: The 10 गण (Gaṇa) — Verb Classes

Resolved to elucidate Sanskrit's ten verb classifications systematically

The 10 गण (Gaṇa) — Verb Classes

The core idea: every dhātu needs a present tense stem before suffixes can attach. The gaṇa tells you how that stem is formed. After the stem is built, the personal endings (-ति, -तः, -न्ति etc.) are the same across all classes.

So: Root → Stem (gaṇa does this) → Add endings (same for all)

The 10 Classes

गणNamed afterWhat it does to the rootExample
1 — भ्वादि√भूAdd -अ to (strengthened) root√भू → भव + ति = भवति
2 — अदादि√अद्Add endings directly — no connecting vowel√अस् → अस् + ति = अस्ति
3 — जुहोत्यादि√हुReduplicate the root first√हु → जु+हो + ति = जुहोति
4 — दिवादि√दिव्Add -य to root√नृत् → नृत्य + ति = नृत्यति
5 — स्वादि√सुAdd -नु/नो to root√सु → सुनो + ति = सुनोति
6 — तुदादि√तुद्Add -अ but without strengthening√तुद् → तुद + ति = तुदति
7 — रुधादि√रुध्Insert -न/न् inside the root√रुध् → रुण+ध् + ति = रुणद्धि
8 — तनादि√तन्Add -उ/ओ to root√तन् → तनो + ति = तनोति
9 — क्र्यादि√क्रीAdd -ना/नी to root√क्री → क्रीणा + ति = क्रीणाति
10 — चुरादि√चुर्Add -अय to (strengthened) root√चुर् → चोरय + ति = चोरयति

The ones that actually matter for Gita reading

Gaṇa 1 (भ्वादि) is by far the largest class — most common dhātus live here. √भू, √गम्, √वद्, √पठ् all follow this pattern. If you learn one gaṇa, learn this one.

Gaṇa 2 (अदादि) has the irregular-feeling verbs — √अस् (to be) and √इ (to go) live here. These are among the most frequent in Sanskrit but behave oddly because they take endings directly.

Gaṇa 10 (चुरादि) is interesting because it's almost a productive class — like English "-ize" (to computerize, to finalize). Many causative and denominative verbs end up here. The -अय- is a giveaway.

Gaṇa 4 (दिवादि) gives you the -य- verbs which are very recognizable — √नृत् becomes नृत्यति (he dances), easy to spot.

The one genuinely tricky thing

Gaṇas 1 and 6 look similar on the surface — both add -अ. The difference is strengthening (guṇa):

  • Gaṇa 1 strengthens the root vowel before adding -अ
  • Gaṇa 6 does not strengthen

So √तुद् (to strike) in gaṇa 6 stays as तुदति — root vowel unchanged. But a gaṇa 1 verb would have its vowel bumped up (अ→आ, इ→ए, उ→ओ etc.).

This strengthening system — called guṇa and vṛddhi — is actually a separate topic worth knowing, because it explains a lot of seemingly random vowel changes across Sanskrit morphology.

The bottom line

You don't memorize all 10 upfront. You encounter a verb, look up its dhātu, the dictionary tells you its gaṇa number, and that tells you how its stem was formed. Over time the patterns become intuitive — especially gaṇas 1, 4, and 10 which cover the majority of what you'll see in the Gita.

You said: Rohan Pandey suggested that he created these images using https://github.

Rohan Pandey suggested that he created these images using https://github.com/ambuda-org/vidyut?tab=readme-ov-file

it is a library which amongst other things can identify a meter of a poem

Now, we know that Awadhi poems don't have the same meter as Sanskrit ones. I was wondering if we could create a similar library that identifies the meter of Awadhi poem. Right now, my ONLY focus is Sundarkand and Ramcharitmanas.

And BTW, is there a way - a library that can show me how to speak these verse/doha/chhand from awadhi

Mar 15

Claude responded: Two separate questions, both have interesting answers.

Synthesized existing meter detection research and identified pronunciation resources

Two separate questions, both have interesting answers.

Part 1: Building an Awadhi Meter Identifier

Good news — the problem is simpler than Sanskrit

Ramcharitmanas uses a small, fixed set of meters. You don't need to detect from hundreds of possibilities like Sanskrit. The meters you'd actually encounter:

MeterStructureWhere used
चौपाई (Chaupai)4 lines, 16 mātrās each, pause at 8Bulk of narrative
दोहा (Doha)2 lines, 13+11 mātrāsTransitional summaries
सोरठा (Soratha)Doha inverted — 11+13Occasional variation
छंद (Chhand)Sanskrit-influenced stanzasInvocations, mangalacharans

That's basically it for Sundarkand. A ~95% coverage detector only needs Chaupai + Doha.

The core algorithm

The key difference from vidyut (which does Sanskrit vṛtta detection): Awadhi uses mātrā counting, not syllable-pattern matching. The rules for mātrā counting are:

  • लघु (short/light) = 1 mātrā — short vowel (अ, इ, उ), followed by at most one consonant
  • गुरु (long/heavy) = 2 mātrās — long vowel (आ, ई, ऊ, ए, ओ, ऐ, औ), or anusvara (ं), or visarga (ः), or followed by a conjunct consonant

Crucially, Awadhi/Prakrit has a different rule from Sanskrit — a laghu before a conjunct consonant in a different word is NOT counted as guru in Prakrit/Awadhi. This is a key deviation you'd need to encode. Infogalactic

There IS existing academic work on this — researchers achieved over 85% accuracy in Chaupai detection, with remaining errors mainly because poets sometimes increase or decrease mātrās to maintain rhythm due to differently structured but similar-sounding words at line endings. IJACSA

What a library would need

Input: Devanagari text string
Step 1: Segment into lines (by ।  ॥ markers)
Step 2: For each line, split into syllables
Step 3: Apply laghu/guru rules (Awadhi variant, not Sanskrit)
Step 4: Count mātrās
Step 5: Check against known patterns:
         - 16 mātrā line → likely Chaupai
         - 13 mātrā line followed by 11 → likely Doha
         - 11+13 → Soratha
Step 6: Validate yati (pause positions) — Chaupai pauses at 8th mātrā

The hardest part is step 2 — Devanagari syllabification, especially handling half-characters (हलन्त), anusvara, and conjuncts correctly. The IJACSA paper linked in the search results has detailed rules for this — worth fetching.

Existing building blocks you could use

  • IIT Kanpur Ramcharitmanas corpus — ramcharitmanas.iitk.ac.in has the full digitized text, cleanly structured by kand/chaupai/doha. Ready-made input data.
  • Indic NLP Library (Python) — handles Devanagari tokenization and has some syllabification support
  • vidyut itself — even though it's Sanskrit-focused, its mātrā counting logic for Devanagari is reusable. The Awadhi-specific rules would be a patch on top.

Part 2: Pronunciation / Recitation Resources

For learning how to say the verses, the best existing resource is:

ramcharitmanas.info — provides audio files word by word, alongside Hindi and English translations, along with a glossary of Awadhi words. This is probably the most directly useful thing for your purpose. Ramcharitmanas

The same site has All India Radio recordings of all seven Kands including Sundarkand — these are the canonical, authoritative recordings for recitation style. Ramcharitmanas

The AIR recordings are particularly good because they follow the traditional पाठ (pāṭh) recitation style — rhythmically consistent with the chaupai meter, not sung/devotional-styled. That's what helps you internalize the beat.

The gap that doesn't yet exist as a tool

What doesn't exist — and would be genuinely useful — is something that shows you the mātrā breakdown of a specific line visually, the way the Gita learning sites you shared do sandhi/anvaya. That would be the actual novel contribution of a library focused on Manas.

You said: What's the verb here?

What's the verb here? कथं भीष्ममहं सङ्ख्ये द्रोणं च मधुसूदन | इषुभि: प्रतियोत्स्यामि पूजार्हावरिसूदन

May 6

Claude responded: प्रतियोत्स्यामि — "I shall fight against"

Deconstructed Sanskrit verb morphology and tense structure

प्रतियोत्स्यामि — "I shall fight against"

Breaking it down

प्रति (prefix, "against/back") + √युध् (to fight) → प्रतियोत्स्यामि

FeatureValue
धातु√युध् — to fight
लकारलृट् — future tense
पुरुषउत्तम (1st person)
वचनएकवचन (singular)

So literally: "I shall fight against"

The full sentence

कथम् = how (the key word that makes this a question)

"How shall I fight against Bhishma and Drona in battle, O Madhusudana?"

The -स्यामि suffix is your future tense signal — same root pattern as करिष्यामि (I shall do), गमिष्यामि (I shall go). Once you see -ष्यामि / -स्यामि at the end, it's almost always लृट् लकार, first person singular.

The emotional weight

This is Arjuna's crisis — he's not saying "I won't fight", he's asking "how can I fight?" The verb form itself (future tense + कथम्) captures the paralysis perfectly. He's not refusing, he's unable to imagine it.

You said: Rewind the verb thingy.

Rewind the verb thingy. Like, I I want to refresh my memory of what we talked about.

May 6
2 / 2

Claude responded: Earlier in this conversation we covered:

Excavated prior verb system discussion to reconstruct foundational grammar concepts

Earlier in this conversation we covered:

The 3×3 grid

Every verb form tells you who and how many. 9 slots:

पुरुषएकवचनद्विवचनबहुवचन
प्रथम (he/she/it)करोतिकुरुतःकुर्वन्ति
मध्यम (you)करोषिकुरुथःकुरुथ
उत्तम (I/we)करोमिकुर्वःकुर्मः

⚠️ The flip: Sanskrit's प्रथम = English 3rd person. Counted from outside in.

The लकार system (tenses)

लकारUsage
लट्Present — करोति
लङ्Past — अकरोत्
लृट्Future — करिष्यति
लोट्Imperative — करोतु
विधिलिङ्Should — कुर्यात्

The decode method

When you see a verb, ask:

  1. What's the धातु?
  2. What लकार? (which tense/mood)
  3. What पुरुष + वचन? (who's doing it, how many)

प्रतियोत्स्यामि — -स्यामि ending → लृट् (future) + उत्तम पुरुष + एकवचन = "I shall..."

You said: In the verse that I mentioned, I get the verb but then how do I determine the subject and the object?

In the verse that I mentioned, I get the verb but then how do I determine the subject and the object? I mean, Ari Sudan I get, but why is it fight against Bhishmaa and not Ari Sudhan?

May 6

Claude responded: This is exactly where विभक्ति earns its keep.

Analyzed Sanskrit case endings to distinguish grammatical subjects from objects

This is exactly where विभक्ति earns its keep. Look at the endings:

The endings tell the whole story

WordEndingविभक्तिRole
भीष्मम्-म्द्वितीया (accusative)Object — being fought
द्रोणं-म्द्वितीया (accusative)Object — being fought
अहं—प्रथमा (nominative)Subject — doing the fighting
मधुसूदन-असम्बोधन (vocative)Address — "O Krishna"
अरिसूदन-असम्बोधन (vocative)Address — "O Krishna" again
इषुभिः-भिःतृतीया (instrumental)"with arrows"

So why not Arisudana?

Because मधुसूदन and अरिसूदन are both vocatives — they're not participating in the action at all. They're just Arjuna calling out to Krishna. Two different epithets of Krishna, both in सम्बोधन.

अरिसूदन = अरि (enemy) + सूदन (slayer) = "O slayer of enemies" — almost ironic here, Arjuna is asking the enemy-slayer how he is supposed to slay people he reveres.

The sentence parsed

अहं (I) कथं (how) भीष्मम् द्रोणं च (Bhishma and Drona) इषुभिः (with arrows) प्रतियोत्स्यामि (shall fight against) — O Madhusudana, O Arisudana?

The -म् ending on Bhishma and Drona is doing all the work. No -म्, no object.

You said: What's the verb here?

What's the verb here?

गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान् श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके | हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव भुञ्जीय भोगान् रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् ||

I suspect it is भोक्तुं

May 6

Claude responded: Close — but भोक्तुं is actually an infinitive, not a finite verb.

Disambiguated infinitive from finite optative verb form

Close — but भोक्तुं is actually an infinitive, not a finite verb. It means "to enjoy/eat" — same role as English "to eat." It can't stand alone as the main verb.

The actual verb is भुञ्जीय.

भुञ्जीय

√भुज् (to enjoy, to eat)

FeatureValue
धातु√भुज्
लकारविधिलिङ् — the "should/ought to" mood
पुरुषप्रथम, एकवचन

"One should enjoy" — that optative mood is key, it's not a statement of fact but of what would be preferable.

The other non-verbs to notice

  • हत्वा — absolutive of √हन् (to kill) = "having killed" — acts like a participle, not a finite verb
  • अहत्वा — same with negative = "without killing"
  • भोक्तुं — infinitive of √भुज् = "to enjoy"

All three are verb-derived but not finite verbs — they can't anchor a sentence on their own.

The meaning

"Better to live on alms in this world without killing these noble gurus, than to enjoy pleasures smeared in blood by having killed them — this is what one should enjoy?"

Arjuna's anguish is grammatically encoded in विधिलिङ् — it's not "I will enjoy" (confident future) but "should one enjoy?" — a horrified rhetorical.

You said: Do a padach chheda as well for this?

Do a padach chheda as well for this?

May 6

Claude responded: पदच्छेद — Splitting the Sandhi

Methodically deconstructed Sanskrit verse into constituent word components

पदच्छेद — Splitting the Sandhi

Line 1: गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान्

गुरून् + अहत्वा + हि + महा + अनुभावान्

  • गुरून् + अहत्वा: न् before vowel, they join in writing
  • महा + अनुभावान्: आ + अ → आ (standard sandhi)

Line 2: श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके

श्रेयः + भोक्तुम् + भैक्ष्यम् + अपि + इह + लोके

  • श्रेयः → श्रेयो: visarga before voiced consonant भ
  • अपि + इह → अपीह: इ + इ → ई

Line 3: हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव

हत्वा + अर्थकामान् + तु + गुरून् + इह + एव

  • हत्वा + अर्थ → हत्वार्थ: आ + अ → आ
  • कामान् + तु → कामांस्तु: न् before त् → ंस्
  • इह + एव → इहैव: अ + ए → ऐ

Line 4: भुञ्जीय भोगान् रुधिरप्रदिग्धान्

भुञ्जीय + भोगान् + रुधिर + प्रदिग्धान्

  • रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् = रुधिर (blood) + प्र + दिग्ध (smeared, from √दिह्) + आन् (accusative plural ending)

अन्वय — Logical Reordering

अहम् महानुभावान् गुरून् अहत्वा इह लोके भैक्ष्यम् भोक्तुम् श्रेयः हि —

हत्वा अर्थकामान् गुरून् इहैव रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् भोगान् भुञ्जीय?

Word by word

पदMeaningविभक्ति
गुरून्gurusद्वितीया, बहुवचन — object
अहत्वाwithout killingअ + absolutive of √हन्
महानुभावान्noble/great-souledद्वितीया, बहुवचन — qualifying गुरून्
श्रेयःbetter/preferablenominative predicate
भोक्तुम्to eat/enjoyinfinitive of √भुज्
भैक्ष्यम्alms, begged foodद्वितीया — object of भोक्तुम्
इहhere (in this world)indeclinable
लोकेin the worldसप्तमी — locative
हत्वाhaving killedabsolutive of √हन्
अर्थकामान्those desiring wealthद्वितीया — object
भोगान्pleasuresद्वितीया — object of भुञ्जीय
रुधिरप्रदिग्धान्smeared in bloodद्वितीया — qualifying भोगान्
भुञ्जीयshould one enjoyविधिलिङ् — main verb

The emotional architecture is visible in the grammar — श्रेयः (better) sits right after अहत्वा (without killing), making the comparison stark before the verb even arrives.

You said: अहम् महानुभावान् गुरून् अहत्वा इह लोके भैक्ष्यम् भोक्तुम् श्रेयः हि —

अहम् महानुभावान् गुरून् अहत्वा इह लोके भैक्ष्यम् भोक्तुम् श्रेयः हि — हत्वा अर्थकामान् गुरून् इहैव रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् भोगान् भुञ्जीय?

In the anvaya, where is the अहम् coming from?

Whats the verb here? अहत्वा, doesn't seem heavy enough

And in Sanskrit as the words can be in any order why did you choose this particular SOV/SVO order

May 6

Claude responded: Three sharp questions, let me be honest about each.

Acknowledged implicit subjects, distinguished finite verbs from gerunds, justified Sanskrit word order conventions

Three sharp questions, let me be honest about each.

1. Where is अहम् coming from?

It's not in the verse. I supplied it from context — Arjuna is the one speaking throughout this chapter so I assumed the subject. That was sloppy of me. The verse doesn't explicitly say अहम्. भुञ्जीय (विधिलिङ्) is impersonal enough to mean "should one enjoy" rather than specifically "should I enjoy." I shouldn't have inserted it without flagging it as implied.

2. The verb in the first half

You're right to push back. The first half —

गुरून् अहत्वा भैक्ष्यम् भोक्तुम् श्रेयः हि

— has no explicit finite verb at all. श्रेयः is a predicate adjective ("preferable/better") with an implied अस्ति (is). This is very common in Sanskrit — the copula gets dropped entirely.

So the structure is:

  • भोक्तुम् — infinitive (to eat)
  • श्रेयः — predicate ("is better")
  • अस्ति — implied, not written

अहत्वा is just an absolutive doing supporting work. The full verb of the whole verse is भुञ्जीय in the second half, which the first half sets up as contrast.

3. Why that particular order in अन्वय

Convention, not logic. Traditional Sanskrit अन्वय always follows Subject → qualifiers → Object → Verb regardless of original order. It's a pedagogical standard, not a claim that Sanskrit "prefers" this order. The original verse scrambles everything for metre — अन्वय just unscrambles it into the most parseable sequence for a student.

You said: Okay, so we don't follow the english SVO for anvaya?

Okay, so we don't follow the english SVO for anvaya?

May 6

Claude responded: Correct.

Clarified Sanskrit word order distinction from English convention

Correct. अन्वय follows SOV — the natural Sanskrit (and Hindi) order.

Subject → Object → Verb

Which is also why Hindi feels natural for this — "मैं सेब खाता हूँ" is SOV, same logic.

The अन्वय convention is:

Subject + (all qualifiers of subject) + Object + (all qualifiers of object) + Verb

So the whole point of अन्वय is to take whatever scrambled order the poet used for metre/rhyme, and restore it to the natural Sanskrit SOV that a reader's mind expects.

You said: What is the correct verb here?

What is the correct verb here? We have so many verbs. What is the correct one? The right one? The one which we'll pick while doing the padachheda and anvaya

May 6

Claude responded: भुञ्जीय — the only finite verb in the verse.

Identified the sole finite verb among multiple verbal forms

भुञ्जीय — the only finite verb in the verse.

The rule is simple: in पदच्छेद and अन्वय, the verb you're looking for is always the finite verb — the one that is actually conjugated for person, number, and tense/mood.

The others are disqualified because:

WordTypeWhy not the main verb
अहत्वाabsolutiveno person/tense — just "without killing"
हत्वाabsolutivesame — "having killed"
भोक्तुम्infinitiveno person/tense — just "to enjoy"
भुञ्जीयfinite verb✓ has person + number + mood

The finite verb is always your anchor. Everything else in the sentence — absolutives, infinitives, participles — orbits around it.

You said: Now do the anvaya and english translation back to back

Now do the anvaya and english translation back to back

May 6

Claude responded: अन्वय:

Parsed Sanskrit verse, constructed grammatical analysis, formulated translation

अन्वय: महानुभावान् गुरून् अहत्वा इह लोके भैक्ष्यम् अपि भोक्तुम् श्रेयः हि — अर्थकामान् गुरून् हत्वा इहैव रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् भोगान् भुञ्जीय?

English: Indeed, not killing these noble gurus, to eat even alms here in this world is better — having killed gurus who desire wealth, should one enjoy pleasures smeared in blood right here?

Okay give the anvaya and the Hindi tranaslaw because I dont want to jump between the SOV of Sanskrit and SVO of English

Claude responded: अन्वय:

Recognized user preference for SOV language pairing

अन्वय: महानुभावान् गुरून् अहत्वा इह लोके भैक्ष्यम् अपि भोक्तुम् श्रेयः हि — अर्थकामान् गुरून् हत्वा इहैव रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् भोगान् भुञ्जीय?

You said: From https://bvsiitm.

राम-शब्दः · a-stem masculine declension (singular)

Prathamā प्रथमा

Dvitīyā द्वितीया

Tṛtīyā तृतीया

Caturthī चतुर्थी

Pañcamī पञ्चमी

Ṣaṣṭhī षष्ठी

Saptamī सप्तमी

Sambodh. सम्बोधन Form रामः — — — — — रामे — Meaning subject — — — — — in Rāma —

We will fill this table across lessons — one or two cases at a time.

From this, we are missing the singular,dual,plural right?

This should have been रामः रामौ रामाः ... रामे ... रामान् or something along those lines

May 6

Claude responded: Yes exactly.

Reconstructed complete Sanskrit declension table with dual and plural forms

Yes exactly. The lesson is deliberately showing only singular for now, filling one or two cases per lesson. But the full table is 8 × 3 = 24 forms.

Here's what it should eventually look like:

विभक्तिएकवचनद्विवचनबहुवचन
प्रथमारामःरामौरामाः
द्वितीयारामम्रामौरामान्
तृतीयारामेणरामाभ्याम्रामैः
चतुर्थीरामायरामाभ्याम्रामेभ्यः
पञ्चमीरामात्रामाभ्याम्रामेभ्यः
षष्ठीरामस्यरामयोःरामाणाम्
सप्तमीरामेरामयोःरामेषु
सम्बोधनरामरामौरामाः

This is exactly your रूपावलि's देवः देवौ देवाः table — same paradigm, just with राम instead of देव. The endings are identical because both are अकारान्त पुल्लिंग (a-stem masculine).

Notice चतुर्थी and पञ्चमी share the same द्विवचन form (रामाभ्याम्) — one of those painful-to-memorize coincidences from your school days.

You said: So noun is धातू and verb is लकार - anything else to add?

So noun is धातू and verb is लकार - anything else to add?

May 6

Claude responded: Actually this is swapped — small but important correction:

Identified and rectified user's reversed grammatical terminology

Actually this is swapped — small but important correction:

धातु is the verb root — √कृ, √भू, √युध् etc.

लकार is the tense/mood applied to that verb root — लट्, लङ्, लृट् etc.

For nouns, the equivalent pair is:

प्रातिपदिक — the noun base/stem (राम, देव, फल)

विभक्ति — the case applied to that stem (प्रथमा, द्वितीया etc.)

So the parallel is clean:

BaseInflection system
Verbधातु (√कृ)लकार (लट्/लङ् etc.)
Nounप्रातिपदिक (राम)विभक्ति (प्रथमा/द्वितीया etc.)

प्रातिपदिक is a term you likely never encountered in school — the रूपावलि just gave you the finished forms without naming the base.

You said: For this give all the 4 together

For this give all the 4 together Original verse Anvaya Hindi English

**अन्वय:** महानुभावान् गुरून् अहत्वा इह लोके भैक्ष्यम् अपि भोक्तुम् श्रेयः हि — अर्थकामान् गुरून् हत्वा इहैव रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् भोगान् भुञ्जीय?

---

**हिंदी:** इन महानुभाव गुरुओं को बिना मारे, इस लोक में भिक्षा का अन्न भी खाना श्रेयस्कर है — अर्थ और काम में लिप्त इन गुरुओं को मारकर, रक्त से सने हुए भोगों को यहीं भोगना चाहिए क्या?

May 6

Claude responded: मूल (Original):

Identified user's request for four textual components together

मूल (Original): गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान् श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके | हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव भुञ्जीय भोगान् रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् ||

अन्वय: महानुभावान् गुरून् अहत्वा इह लोके भैक्ष्यम् अपि भोक्तुम् श्रेयः हि — अर्थकामान् गुरून् हत्वा इहैव रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् भोगान् भुञ्जीय?

हिंदी: इन महानुभाव गुरुओं को बिना मारे, इस लोक में भिक्षा का अन्न भी खाना श्रेयस्कर है — अर्थ और काम में लिप्त इन गुरुओं को मारकर, रक्त से सने हुए भोगों को यहीं भोगना चाहिए क्या?

English: Indeed, not killing these noble gurus, to eat even alms here in this world is better — having killed gurus who desire wealth and pleasure, should one enjoy pleasures smeared in blood right here?

You said: What makes it महानुभावान् गुरून् i.

What makes it महानुभावान् गुरून् i.e. महानुभाव गुरुओं rather than 2 separate words महानुभाव, गुरुओं

May 6

Claude responded: Because they agree in विभक्ति + वचन + लिंग.

Identified grammatical agreement binding adjective and noun together

Because they agree in विभक्ति + वचन + लिंग.

Both are:

  • द्वितीया (accusative — objects of अहत्वा)
  • बहुवचन (plural)
  • पुल्लिंग (masculine)

महानुभावान् is an adjective qualifying गुरून्. In Sanskrit, an adjective must match its noun in all three — case, number, gender. That matching is what tells you they belong together.

If they were two separate objects they would still both be द्वितीया, but the meaning of महानुभाव would have to stand alone as a noun ("noble ones") rather than as a descriptor of गुरु.

The way you know it's an adjective and not a separate noun here is context + the -आन् ending — महानुभाव means "of great spirit/noble," which is naturally an adjective quality, not a standalone entity. गुरु is the actual noun being referred to throughout the verse.

This adjectival agreement is also why रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् भोगान् goes together later in the verse — same logic, रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् (smeared in blood) agrees with भोगान् (pleasures), both द्वितीया बहुवचन.

You said: Is this some rule?

Is this some rule?

May 6

Claude responded: Yes — it's called विशेषण-विशेष्य agreement (adjective-noun agreement).

Yes — it's called विशेषण-विशेष्य agreement (adjective-noun agreement).

The rule: an adjective (विशेषण) must match its noun (विशेष्य) in all three:

  • लिंग (gender)
  • वचन (number)
  • विभक्ति (case)

This is actually what makes Sanskrit so different from English. In English adjectives never change:

"noble guru" / "noble gurus" — noble stays noble

In Sanskrit the adjective changes form to match the noun every time:

NounAdjective must become
गुरुः (nom. sg.)महानुभावः
गुरून् (acc. pl.)महानुभावान्
गुरुभिः (inst. pl.)महानुभावैः

This is also why Sanskrit is hard to read without this rule — when you see two words far apart in a verse, if their endings agree in all three, they belong together even if separated by several other words. The poet can scramble word order freely for metre, and the endings still tell you what goes with what.

It's the same principle as विभक्ति doing the grammatical work — just extended to adjectives.

You said: Sometimes you use prathama, sambodhan and sometimes accusative

Sometimes you use prathama, sambodhan and sometimes accusative

Ek cheej use karo yaar

May 6

Claude responded: Bilkul sahi pakda.

Recognized inconsistency, resolved to standardize terminology choice

Bilkul sahi pakda. My bad.

Which do you want — Sanskrit names or English names?

Sanskrit: प्रथमा, द्वितीया, तृतीया... English: nominative, accusative, instrumental...

Sanskrit is better long-term since that's what the Gita learning sites use. But your call.

You said: Yeah for the गुरू explanation.

Yeah for the गुरू explanation. Please expand on it. Make it simpler but consistent

May 6

Claude responded: विशेषण-विशेष्य agreement

Restructured Sanskrit grammar explanation with clearer terminology

विशेषण-विशेष्य agreement

When an adjective describes a noun in Sanskrit, it must match the noun in all three:

  • लिंग (gender)
  • वचन (number)
  • विभक्ति (case)

गुरून् is:

  • पुल्लिंग
  • बहुवचन
  • द्वितीया (object of अहत्वा — "without killing whom?")

महानुभावान् is also:

  • पुल्लिंग
  • बहुवचन
  • द्वितीया

All three match → they belong together. महानुभावान् is describing गुरून्, not standing alone.

Same thing later in the verse:

भोगान् is पुल्लिंग, बहुवचन, द्वितीया

रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् is also पुल्लिंग, बहुवचन, द्वितीया

→ "pleasures smeared in blood" — same logic, same rule.

In English adjectives never change form — "noble guru", "noble gurus", noble stays noble. In Sanskrit the adjective changes its ending every time to mirror the noun. That changing ending is the signal that says these two words belong together.

You said: But why is it an adjective, it can be noun as well right?

But why is it an adjective, it can be noun as well right?महानुभावान्

May 6

Claude responded: Correct — महानुभावान् can absolutely stand as a noun meaning "noble ones.

Acknowledged the valid question about Sanskrit grammatical ambiguity

Correct — महानुभावान् can absolutely stand as a noun meaning "noble ones."

The ending alone doesn't tell you. What tells you is a combination of two things:

1. Meaning/context महानुभाव means "of great spirit" — it's a quality, which naturally makes it adjective-like. And the verse is clearly about gurus throughout, so गुरून् is the established noun.

2. They are in apposition Both are द्वितीया बहुवचन पुल्लिंग — they sit right next to each other referring to the same people. Sanskrit commonly stacks a noun + descriptive noun/adjective this way.

Actually the honest answer is — you can read it either way:

  • "without killing the noble-ones, the gurus" → महानुभावान् as separate noun
  • "without killing the noble gurus" → महानुभावान् as adjective

Both are grammatically valid. Traditional commentators generally treat it as adjective qualifying गुरून् — one unified idea, not two separate groups.

This is actually a real challenge in Sanskrit reading — the grammar alone doesn't always settle the question. Context, commentary tradition, and meaning together make the call. This is exactly why टीका literature exists.

You said: Is this some rule?

Is this some rule? Like one of paninian rules? IDK, I am noob. Help me explain

May 6

Claude responded: Yes — the underlying concept is called सामानाधिकरण्य (sāmānādhikaraṇya) in Paninian grammar.

Investigated whether adjective-noun agreement stems from Paninian grammar rules

Yes — the underlying concept is called सामानाधिकरण्य (sāmānādhikaraṇya) in Paninian grammar.

It means roughly "same-referentiality" — two words referring to the same entity must agree in case.

Panini's logic is simple: if two words point to the same thing in the world, their grammatical endings must match. The matching endings are the signal that they co-refer.

So it's not specifically an "adjective rule" — it's deeper than that. It covers:

  • Adjective + noun (महानुभावान् गुरून्)
  • Noun + noun in apposition ("Ram the king" — रामो राजा)
  • Predicate agreement

All the same principle — same referent → same ending.

The practical takeaway for reading

When you're doing पदच्छेद and see two words with matching लिंग + वचन + विभक्ति, Panini is essentially telling you — these two words are talking about the same thing. Group them together.

It's what makes Sanskrit readable despite scrambled word order. The endings create invisible threads connecting words across a verse, regardless of where the poet placed them for metre.

You're not a noob for asking this — this is actually the conceptual heart of Sanskrit grammar. Most school Sanskrit never explains why the endings matter, only what they are.

You said: How many such paninian rules are there?

How many such paninian rules are there?

May 6

Claude responded: Panini's अष्टाध्यायी (Ashtadhyayi) has approximately 3,959 सूत्र (sutras) — rules.

Recalled established fact about Paninian sutras

Panini's अष्टाध्यायी (Ashtadhyayi) has approximately 3,959 सूत्र (sutras) — rules.

That number is why I'd stop you from going down that rabbit hole right now. It's an entire formal grammar system — linguists and Sanskrit scholars spend careers on it.

For reading the Gita, you don't need Panini directly. What you're doing right now is the right approach — encounter a verse, ask why something works the way it does, learn the concept behind it. सामानाधिकरण्य came up naturally because you pushed on महानुभावान् गुरून्.

That's essentially how traditional Sanskrit learning worked too — not "read Panini first, then read texts" but "read texts, and when something needs explaining, trace it back to the rule."

Panini is the source code. You're reading the output. You only need to look at the source when the output confuses you.

Just to give you some context, I have had a conversation with Claude about the project. So I was using another tool called as conductor dot bill using which I created a project called as our data reader. However, it is a little tangential to what we are talking about right now. But it is related. So what I'm trying to say is I have some markdown files. I would like you to read it and get some idea about where I'm coming from And as I mentioned, I would like to understand more of this more of this. Right? So whatever we were talking about. Idea behind this is I want to learn Sanskrit faster, and I have some resources that are going to help me. I have found that people suggest that Gita is the best way to learn Sanskrit. I don't want to boil the ocean at the same time. But I want to learn bits and pieces of it. So, yeah, I'll give you some context. And have a look at it and tell me what you can. Figure out from it. And yeah, just just have it in your memory, in your brain, in your run time memory.

Claude responded: Read it.

Absorbed contextual file and acknowledged Sanskrit learning project

Read it. It's essentially a transcript of exactly what we've been doing in this conversation — so I'm fully up to speed.

The key things I'm holding:

Where you're coming from:

  • Maharashtra SSC Sanskrit background — noun ratta, no verb understanding
  • Interest in languages, not actively trying to "learn Sanskrit" in a formal sense
  • Gita as the vehicle, specifically the bvsiitm.github.io lessons
  • Bibek Debroy's book as prior context

What you've picked up in this conversation:

  • धातु → लकार for verbs, प्रातिपदिक → विभक्ति for nouns
  • पदच्छेद and अन्वय as the two-step decode method
  • Finite verb as the anchor
  • विशेषण-विशेष्य agreement, सामानाधिकरण्य
  • The Ramcharitmanas/Awadhi meter project interest

The project thread:

  • Awadhi mātrā-based meter detector for Sundarkand
  • IIT Kanpur corpus as data source
  • ramcharitmanas.info as pronunciation resource

One thing I notice — the file has the conductor.bill project mentioned but the actual content of that project isn't in this file. If that's relevant to the meter/library project, you'd need to share those notes separately.

What do you want to continue with?

You said: Just to give you some more context here is the project that I made

yadavvi91/awadhi-meter-identifier

yadavvi91/awadhi-meter-detect

GITHUB

Just to give you some more context here is the project that I made

May 7

Claude responded: This is genuinely impressive work.

Absorbed project codebase to contextualize Awadhi Meter Visualizer

This is genuinely impressive work. Let me reflect back what I'm seeing so we're on the same page.

What you built

A React + Vite web app that does exactly what we discussed — visually breaks down Ramcharitmanas verses into laghu/guru syllables and shows where the यति (singing pause) falls. The 4-step animated widget mirrors the bvsiitm.github.io interaction model you liked.

The engine (syllabify.js) is the real work — it handles:

  • Devanagari syllabification with conjuncts, virama, anusvara, chandrabindu
  • The Awadhi-specific rule (cross-word conjunct doesn't make preceding syllable guru)
  • Diphthong merging (चलेउ → ["च", "लेउ"])
  • Aspiration exclusion (म्ह, न्ह not treated as conjuncts)

The corpus is all 60 sections of Sundarkand from the IIT Kanpur text, with soratha sections correctly re-tagged from what IITK mislabeled as doha.

Accuracy from the checkpoints: ~71.7% for chaupai, ~38% for doha — with the honest doc explaining why (recited Awadhi vs. written spelling).

The honest open problem

The docs/awadhi-prosody.md file is the most intellectually interesting part. You've correctly identified that the engine counts from spelling, but Tulsidas wrote for recitation where:

  • कीन्हें compresses to 3 mātrās not 4
  • श्र simplifies to स्र
  • Rhythmic resolution allows 2L ↔ 1G

The yati proportional scaling workaround is pragmatic — it gets the pause in the right word even when the total is off.

What's missing / what's next?

The CHECKPOINT-2 ends at UI polish and engine revert. A few natural next directions:

  • GitHub Pages deployment — mentioned in CLAUDE.md but not done yet
  • The mātrā counting doc (matra-counting-rules.md) suggests a vowel-only counting mode for doha/soratha that you tried and reverted — worth revisiting as an optional mode rather than default
  • Audio integration — ramcharitmanas.info word-by-word audio could be linked per verse

What's the immediate thing you want to work on?

Synthesized grammar rules and Gita verses into comprehensive Sanskrit learning reference

The site is a React SPA so it won't fetch cleanly. But I have everything we discussed in this conversation — let me compile it all into a reference doc now.

Viewed a file, created a file, read a file

The document has 6 parts:

  1. The core idea — the two-pillar system (nouns + verbs), and the three-step decode (पदच्छेद → अन्वय → translation)
  2. Nouns — all 8 विभक्ति, 3 वचन, 3 लिंग, full राम declension table
  3. Adjectives — विशेषण-विशेष्य agreement, सामानाधिकरण्य, with examples from our Gita verses
  4. Verbs — धातु table, 3×3 grid, 5 key लकार with suffix shortcuts, non-finite forms, all 10 गण
  5. Worked examples — all 4 verses we analysed (Gita 1.47, 2.4, 2.5, and वृन्दावने कृष्णः), each with मूल → पदच्छेद → अन्वय → हिंदी → English
  6. Quick reference cards — ending patterns for case spotting, tense spotting, and the three-step verb decode

The hindi translation is always before English, keeping you in SOV mode. What would you like to add or change?

What the explainer.html revealed:

  • The course opens with धृतराष्ट्र उवाच as the anchor verse — chosen because it encodes all four concepts (vibhakti, lakāra, dhātu, sandhi) in just two words
  • The Known → +1 → Drill sequence — sandhi deliberately comes last because you need to know the expected form before a rule about how it got mangled makes sense
  • The 192 dhātu / 95% Gita coverage insight — this is the same corpus data behind Rohan Pandey's image
  • The √वच् family (उवाच, ऊचुः, उक्त्वा, उच्यते, वक्ष्यामि, वचनम्, वाच्) as a concrete demonstration of why dhātu-first learning is generative, not rote

You said: Tangential: (i.

Tangential: (i.e. do NOT mess my data already created) - are the verbs done like 3x3 * 5 (i.e. verb conjugation * tenses) (like the noun is 8 * 3 (vibhakti * numbers)

May 7

Claude responded: Yes, exactly that parallel.

Yes, exactly that parallel.

Nouns: 8 विभक्ति × 3 वचन = 24 forms per stem

Verbs: 9 forms (3 पुरुष × 3 वचन) × 10 लकार = 90 forms per dhātu

You said 5 लकार — that's the practical Gita-reading set (लट्, लङ्, लृट्, लोट्, विधिलिङ्). But Sanskrit formally has 10 लकार total. The other 5 you'd rarely encounter in the Gita:

लकारUsage
लिट्Remote/narrative perfect — उवाच is this one
लुट्Periphrastic future ("will eventually")
लृङ्Conditional ("would have")
आशीर्लिङ्Benedictive ("may he...")
लुङ्Aorist past (another past tense)

So the full picture is 3 × 3 × 10 = 90 forms per dhātu, but realistically for the Gita you need maybe 3 × 3 × 6 = 54 (your 5 + लिट् since उवाच shows up constantly).

One wrinkle the noun system doesn't have: verbs also split into परस्मैपद / आत्मनेपद — roughly "active" vs "reflexive/middle" voice. Each has its own 9-form set. So the theoretical maximum is 3 × 3 × 10 × 2 = 180 forms per dhātu. This is what made the रूपावलि धातु रूप section so painful.

You said: so why not make the verb thingy more clear with 3 x 3 * 5 table?

so why not make the verb thingy more clear with 3 x 3 * 5 table? at least for one √कृ like you did for राम (do not delete anything - add or append only)

May 7

Viewed 2 files, edited 2 files, read a file

Done. Added 5 separate 3×3 tables for √कृ — one per लकार — inserted right after the existing single लट् table, before the summary लकार list. Nothing deleted.

Each table has a callout connecting back to a verse you've already seen — अकुर्वत from 1.47, करिष्यामि showing the -ष्य- marker, भुञ्जीय from 2.5. So the tables are anchored to actual Gita examples rather than floating abstractly.

Claude responded: On your Mac, the file is downloading to your system.

Identified default app issue and macOS configuration solution

On your Mac, the file is downloading to your system. A few ways:

Quickest — drag to VSCode: Download the file, then drag it onto the VSCode icon in your dock.

Set VSCode as default for .md files permanently:

  1. Find any .md file in Finder
  2. Right-click → Get Info
  3. Under Open With → select Visual Studio Code
  4. Click Change All...

Done — every .md file opens in VSCode from then on.

From terminal (since you're a terminal person):

bash
code ~/Downloads/sanskrit-reference.md

Assuming code is in your PATH — if not, in VSCode do Cmd+Shift+P → "Shell Command: Install 'code' command in PATH" once, and you're set forever.

Claude responded: Ah — that's the Claude.

Ah — that's the Claude.ai file presentation UI, not your system. That's controlled by Anthropic, not something I can change from my end.

Your options:

  1. Google Drive button — upload it there, then download and open in VSCode locally
  2. Copy the raw content — ask me to print the markdown and copy-paste it into a new VSCode file yourself
  3. Download it — there should be a download option somewhere in that UI, then open with VSCode using any of the methods from my previous message

The Antigravity integration is whatever Anthropic has set up as the default editor connector. VSCode isn't currently one of the connected options in the Claude.ai interface.

Claude responded: Honestly — I don't know.

Honestly — I don't know. Let me check.

Searched the web

Short answer: there is no setting in Claude.ai to swap Antigravity for VSCode. Antigravity is Google's IDE and it's what Anthropic has integrated as the default "open in editor" option in the file UI. VSCode is not a connected option there.

Your actual options:

  1. Download the file from the Claude UI (should be a download option somewhere near that button), then code ~/Downloads/sanskrit-reference.md in terminal
  2. Google Drive button → downloads to Drive → grab from there
  3. Ask me to print the raw content in chat → copy-paste into a new file in VSCode yourself

None of these are as clean as a direct "Open in VSCode" button, but that button simply doesn't exist in Claude.ai right now. If you want it, the thumbs down on that file UI and a feature request to Anthropic is the right path.
